title: Antiferromagnetic order and magnetic polarons in lightly doped Li$_x$CoO$_2$ (x $\sim$ 0.9)
authors: Sudip Pal, Pampa Sadhukhan, Waqar Suleman, Andrej Pustogow, S. B. Roy
abstract: We investigate the magnetic properties of Li$_x$CoO$_2$ (x$\sim$0.9) using bulk magnetization, specific heat, nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy measurements. The dc magnetization, specific heat and NMR measurements, which probe the macroscopic response, indeed show that this compound partially undergoes an antiferromagnetic transition below $T_N \sim$ 10 K. In addition, we observed a weak ferromagnetic response, which gives rise to the history dependence in magnetization measurements at low fields and is observed at temperatures above room temperature. We propose that there are ferromagnetic clusters at high temperatures due to the formation of magnetic polarons out of doped holes. In EPR measurements performed at the $X$-band frequency, only a fraction of the total spins contribute and show Curie-like paramagnetic behavior as reflected in the temperature dependence of the EPR intensity. The temperature variation of the EPR spectra can be understood in the framework of the diffusion of magnetic polarons.
summary: This work investigates the magnetism of $	ext{Li}_x	ext{CoO}_2$ by combining magnetization, specific heat, NMR, and EPR measurements. The findings reveal evidence for both weak antiferromagnetic ordering at low temperatures and a persistent weak ferromagnetism arising from magnetic polarons formed by doped holes. The study models these phenomena using polaron hopping dynamics, highlighting the complex interplay between charge carriers, spin correlations, and structural disorder in correlated oxides.
main_problem: The study investigates the complex magnetic properties of lightly doped $	ext{Li}_x	ext{CoO}_2$ ($x \sim 0.9$), aiming to understand the origin of its low-temperature magnetic order and history dependence.
main_result: The material exhibits evidence for weak antiferromagnetic ordering below $T_N \sim 10 	ext{ K}$ alongside a persistent weak ferromagnetic response, which is attributed to the formation and diffusion of localized magnetic polarons.
method: A combination of bulk measurements (magnetization, specific heat), spectroscopic techniques ($	ext{NMR}$, $	ext{EPR}$), and structural analysis ($	ext{XRD}$) were employed across various temperature regimes.
model_or_system: The system is the layered transition metal oxide $	ext{Li}_x	ext{CoO}_2$. The physics involves localized spins interacting via doped holes, leading to the formation of magnetic polarons which govern the magnetic response and transport properties.
key_observables: Magnetization ($	ext{M}$ vs $	ext{T}$, ZFC/FCW), specific heat anomaly near $10 	ext{ K}$, $	ext{NMR}$ spin-lattice relaxation rate ($1/T_1$), and $	ext{EPR}$ signal intensity, linewidth, and $g$-factor.
important_parameters: Low doping concentration ($x \sim 0.9$); low temperature regime ($	ext{T} < 300 	ext{ K}$); magnetic field strength (e.g., $100 	ext{ Oe}$, $9 	ext{ T}$).
main_assumptions: The weak ferromagnetism and history dependence are linked to the formation of magnetic polarons; the low-temperature order is modeled as an antiferromagnetic transition in hole clusters.
figures_summary: Figures show $	ext{XRD}$ confirming structure, $	ext{M}-	ext{T}$ curves revealing ZFC/FCW irreversibility and anomalies near $10 	ext{ K}$, and $	ext{EPR}$ spectra demonstrating temperature-dependent signal changes consistent with polaron dynamics.
paper_structure: The paper systematically presents evidence from multiple techniques: first, bulk measurements establish the low-$T$ AFM transition; second, magnetization hysteresis points to short-range ferromagnetism related to polarons; and finally, $	ext{EPR}$ and $	ext{NMR}$ provide microscopic details on polaron dynamics across different temperature regimes.
why_interesting: The interplay between charge localization (polarons), magnetic ordering ($	ext{AFM}/	ext{FM}$ coexistence), and ionic disorder ($	ext{Li}^+$ diffusion) in a correlated oxide system provides rich physics relevant to understanding emergent quantum states in strongly interacting electron systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. XRD pattern of the Li0.9CoO2 powder sample recorded at room temperature using synchrotron x-ray source of wavelength λ = 0.7112 Å (open circles). The red line shows the rietveld refinement of the powder spectra using Fullprof software. Blue line shows the difference between experimen- tal data and the simulated pattern.

FIG. 2. (a) M −T curves measured at µ0H = 100 Oe following the ZFC, FCC and FCW protocols. Inset shows the minima observed around 200 K in the ZFC (b) the low temperature M −T at µ0H = 100 Oe. Inset shows the first derivative of the ZFC to highlight the anomaly at TN (c) It shows M −T curve at µ0H = 1 Tesla, (d) the semi-log plot of σ vs. T −0.25. The red line is the linear fit to the data.
FIG. 4. Isothermal M −µ0H curve at (a) T = 300 K and (b) 2 K.
FIG. 3. The double-log plot of the M −T curves recorded fol- lowing thermal cycles from progressively higher temperatures measured at µ0H = 100 Oe following the (a) ZFC protocol. (b) FC protocol.
FIG. 6. Magnetic field dependence of the heat capacity. At higher fields, the anomaly becomes broader and shifts towards lower temperatures.
